statement.title: Statement

solutions.title: Tutorial
solutions.content:
2203F - Binary Search with One Swap
Let's call the integer $$$\lfloor \frac{l + r}{2} \rfloor$$$ the
center
of the segment $$$[l, r]$$$. We say that the center of the segment
controls
every integer in the segment.
Consider the binary tree that defines the binary search process. The root controls $$$c = \lfloor \frac{1+n}{2} \rfloor$$$ the whole segment $$$[1, n]$$$, the left child of the root controls its left part — the segment $$$[1, c-1]$$$, the right child of the root controls its right part — the segment $$$[c+1, n]$$$, and so on.
Suppose we are swapping $$$a_i$$$ with $$$a_j$$$. Let $$$k$$$ be the LCA of $$$i$$$ and $$$j$$$ in this tree. If an integer $$$x$$$ is not controlled by $$$k$$$, then searching for the integer $$$x$$$ will not go to $$$k$$$ or its subtree; so, every integer outside of $$$k$$$'s subtree will be successfully found.
This allows us to use the following approach: iterate on $$$k$$$ (the LCA of $$$i$$$ and $$$j$$$), and consider only the subtree of $$$k$$$ as integers which might not be found. For this fixed $$$k$$$, we are going to build an array $$$[p_0, p_1, \dots, p_{f(k)}]$$$, where $$$f(k)$$$ is the size of the segment that $$$k$$$ controls, and $$$p_x$$$ is the number of pairs $$$(i, j)$$$ such that their LCA is $$$k$$$ and swapping them makes exactly $$$x$$$ elements impossible to find.
If $$$k$$$ is the LCA of $$$i$$$ and $$$j$$$, then there are three possible cases for the location of $$$i$$$ and $$$j$$$:
$$$i$$$ is in the left subtree, $$$j = k$$$;
$$$i = k$$$, $$$j$$$ is in the right subtree;
$$$i$$$ is in the left subtree, $$$j$$$ is in the right subtree.
We will consider the first and the third case; the second case is symmetric to the first one and can be solved in the same way.
Case $$$1$$$: let's say that we swap $$$k$$$ with $$$i$$$, and $$$i$$$ belongs to the left subtree of $$$k$$$. Which integers won't be found? I claim that these are all integers from $$$i+1$$$ to $$$k$$$: we will go to the right subtree of $$$k$$$ while searching for them (since the vertex $$$k$$$ now has value $$$i$$$), but they are all in the left subtree. For all integers less than $$$i$$$ or greater than $$$k$$$, nothing will change, and for $$$i$$$ itself, it will be found as soon as we arrive to the vertex $$$k$$$. So, the number of integers we won't find if we swap $$$k$$$ with $$$i$$$ is exactly $$$k-i$$$.
Case $$$3$$$: let's say that we swap $$$i$$$ with $$$j$$$, $$$i$$$ belongs to the left subtree of $$$k$$$, and $$$j$$$ belongs to the right subtree of $$$k$$$. Let $$$g(i)$$$ be the number of integers in the left subtree of $$$k$$$ which won't be found if we replace $$$i$$$ with some integer greater than $$$k$$$, and $$$h(j)$$$ be the number of integers in the right subtree of $$$k$$$ which won't be found if we replace $$$j$$$ with some integer less than $$$k$$$. I claim that $$$g(i)$$$ is equal to the size of the right subtree of $$$i$$$, plus one: obviously, $$$i$$$ won't be found; but also, when we arrive at the subtree which $$$i$$$ controlled, we have to go to the right if we are looking for some integer from the right subtree, but we will go to the left instead. Similarly, $$$h(j)$$$ is equal to the size of the left subtree of $$$j$$$, plus one.
This allows us to calculate all of the values of $$$g(i)$$$ and $$$h(j)$$$ in $$$O(n)$$$, but how do we "merge" them? This looks like a FFT problem, but FFT is too slow. Instead, let's analyze the possible values of $$$g(i)$$$ and $$$h(j)$$$. I claim that there are only $$$O(\log n)$$$ different values of $$$g(i)$$$ and $$$h(j)$$$, because there are $$$\log_2 n$$$ layers in our tree and the sizes of subtrees in each layer differ at most by $$$1$$$. So, we can simply build two frequency maps which, for every $$$g(i)$$$ and $$$h(j)$$$, store the number of vertices having this value of $$$g(i)$$$ or $$$h(j)$$$ in the subtree, and merge them naively. The sizes of these maps are at most $$$\log n$$$, so processing every $$$k$$$ works in $$$O(f(k) \log \log n + \log^2 n)$$$.
And our final optimization will be as follows: if two vertices which we choose as LCAs of $$$i$$$ and $$$j$$$ have the same subtree sizes, then the search in their subtrees behaves exactly the same. So we don't need to calculate everything for each possible vertex $$$k$$$; instead, for every $$$f(k)$$$, let's count the number of vertices having this $$$f(k)$$$, calculate the answers for that value of $$$f(k)$$$, and multiply them. There are only $$$O(\log n)$$$ different values of $$$f(k)$$$, and their sum is $$$O(n)$$$, so now our solution works in $$$O(n \log \log n + \log^3 n)$$$.
